sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian state agriculture departments, the Australian apple and pear industry, and horticultural research.

core.intro: Wherever you garden in Australia, the groundwork for a good pear is the same: full sun, a deep and reasonably drained soil, a variety matched to your winter chill, and in most cases a second variety nearby to pollinate it. Two distinct families are on offer, the soft buttery European pears and the crisp Asian nashi, and they are picked and ripened quite differently. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you live; the notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Pears come in two families. European pears (Pyrus communis) are the soft, sweet, buttery pears you ripen indoors: Williams Bon Chretien (called Bartlett in America), Beurre Bosc with its long neck and russet skin, the Australian-bred Packham's Triumph, the winter-keeping Josephine, the small dessert pear sold as Corella, and red-skinned Williams sports sold as Sensation. Asian or nashi pears (Pyrus pyrifolia) are round, and you eat them crisp and juicy off the tree like an apple: Nijisseiki (also sold as Twentieth Century), Shinseiki, Hosui, Chojuro and Ya Li. The deciding factor for most gardens is winter chill. Mainstream European pears want a properly cold winter to flower and fruit well, so for warm, coastal and subtropical gardens choose a nashi or a low-chill European pear such as Flordahome, Hood or Bonza, which crop on only a few hundred chill hours. Work out what your winter can deliver first, then pick a named variety to match.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: Most European pears are self-incompatible, meaning a tree sets little or no fruit with its own pollen and crops properly only when a different variety flowers near it at the same time, within bee range. A few, such as Williams, are partially self-fertile and will set a light crop alone, but even these fruit far better with a partner. Pears also flower early in spring, earlier than apples, so a cold snap or a quiet day for bees at blossom can cut the crop, which is another reason to plant compatible varieties close together. If you have room for only one tree, choose a self-fertile variety such as Sunshine or Bonza, a multi-graft tree that carries several varieties on one trunk, or a self-pollinating nashi like Nijisseiki or Shinseiki. European and Asian pears will happily pollinate each other when their flowering overlaps.

sections.heading: Rootstocks and tree size
sections.body: Like apples, pears are grafted onto a rootstock that sets the tree's eventual size. A vigorous pear (Pyrus) seedling rootstock such as D6 makes a large, long-lived tree that can take several years to come into bearing, while a quince rootstock (Quince A, Quince C or BA29) makes a smaller, earlier-fruiting tree that suits a garden, an espalier or a large pot. Quince has two catches worth knowing: it is less cold-hardy and can struggle on limey soils, and several popular varieties, including Williams Bon Chretien and Packham's Triumph, are graft-incompatible with it, so nurseries either grow those on a pear rootstock or double-work them with a compatible interstem such as Beurre Hardy. For a courtyard, a balcony or a pot, look for a tree on quince or a naturally compact dwarf type such as the Trixzie pears. The main thing for a buyer is to check what rootstock a tree is on, because that, more than anything, decides how big it will get.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Pears want a sunny, open position and a deep soil, and they are more forgiving of heavy and damp ground than most fruit trees, which is one reason they thrived in the old river-flat orchards of the south-east. They still dislike constant waterlogging, so avoid a spot where water truly sits, dig gypsum into heavy clay to open it up, and aim for a slightly acidic to neutral soil around pH 6 to 7. Pears are deciduous and are usually sold and planted bare-rooted in winter while dormant, which is the cheapest way to buy one, though potted trees can go in through much of the year as long as you keep the water up over their first summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Young trees need regular water to establish, and any bearing tree needs steady, even moisture while the fruit is filling, plus a good mulch to hold it. Pears are light to moderate feeders, and nitrogen is usually the main nutrient to add each year, as a complete fertiliser or as compost in late winter to early spring before growth starts. Go easy with it: too much nitrogen drives soft, leafy growth at the expense of fruit, and that lush growth is more prone to pests and to scab in wet districts. A pear in reasonable soil asks for surprisingly little once it is established.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Here is where pears differ from almost every other fruit tree. European pears are picked mature but still hard and ripened off the tree, never left to soften on the branch: a pear left to ripen in place ripens from the inside out and turns brown, soft and gritty at the core before the outside is ready. A pear is ready to pick when its green skin starts to lighten and the fruit parts easily from the spur as you cup it and lift it to horizontal. Ripen the picked fruit at room temperature and test it at the neck, just below the stem, which yields to gentle pressure when it is ready to eat. Summer pears such as Williams ripen within a few days, but winter pears such as Beurre Bosc, Josephine and Winter Nelis need a spell of cold first, a few weeks near 0 to 4 degrees in the fridge or a cold shed, or they shrivel instead of ripening. Pears are climacteric and keep ripening after picking, so sound hard fruit stores for weeks to months somewhere cold and dark. Nashi are the exception that proves the rule: you leave them to ripen on the tree and pick them ready to eat.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than an unnamed seedling, because pears do not come true from seed and a seedling can take a decade or more to fruit, if it ever fruits well. Check the label for three things: the variety, the rootstock (which sets the tree's size), and the pollination needs, and plan a partner from the start, whether a second compatible variety, a multi-graft tree or a self-fertile type. Pears and their pome-fruit cousin the apple share much the same care, pests and pollination habits, so they make natural companions in a garden. The in-stock table above lists the named European and nashi varieties available right now, with sizes and prices, including low-chill types for warm districts and dwarf trees for pots and small gardens.

faqs.q: Why is my pear tree healthy but not fruiting?
faqs.a: The usual reasons are that the tree is still too young, since pears are slower than apples to settle into bearing, especially on a vigorous pear rootstock, or that there is no compatible variety flowering nearby to pollinate it. A heavy crop one year can also bring a light one the next, and a tree given too much nitrogen will grow leaves instead of flowers. Because pears blossom early, a single hard frost at flowering can also cost you that year's fruit.

faqs.q: Do pear trees lose their leaves in winter?
faqs.a: Yes. Pears are deciduous, dropping their leaves in autumn and resting through winter, and it is that cold rest that lets them flower in spring. Bare-rooted trees are sold and planted during this dormant season, which is the cheapest and often the best way to buy one.

faqs.q: Can I grow a pear in a pot or espalier it against a fence?
faqs.a: Yes. Start with a tree on a quince rootstock or a compact dwarf type, use a large container of free-draining mix, water often because pots dry out fast, and feed regularly through the growing season. Pears also take very well to espalier, trained flat against a sunny wall or along wires and summer-pruned to stay neat, which crops heavily for the space and makes netting and picking easy.

faqs.q: Are nashi (Asian) pears easier to grow than European pears?
faqs.a: Often, yes, especially in a warm garden or for a beginner. Nashi take less winter chill, several kinds set a crop without a partner, they shrug off most diseases, and best of all you pick them ripe straight from the branch and bite in like an apple, with none of the indoor ripening a European pear needs.

WA.intro: Growing pears in Western Australia. WA grows European pears in its cool south and crisp nashi in the warmer Swan Valley and Perth foothills, and it shares apples' rare good luck: it is free of codling moth.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: How well pears grow in WA depends on where you are. The Perth Hills and the cooler Southern Forests around Manjimup and Pemberton carry enough winter chill for mainstream European pears, while the warm coastal plain and the Swan Valley are lower in chill and better suited to nashi and low-chill pears. WA's hot, dry summers are an advantage either way: they ripen the late pears fully and keep the fungal diseases of the humid east in check.

sections.heading: Where pears grow in WA
sections.body: Western Australia's pome fruit comes mainly from the cool South West. The Manjimup and Pemberton districts, marketed as the Southern Forests, together with Donnybrook and the Perth Hills, grow apples and pears side by side, served by the same growers and the industry body Pomewest. Nashi do well in the warmer Swan Valley and the Perth Hills, and the Department's research institute at Manjimup has long trialled temperate fruit for these conditions.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's warm summers bring the crop on early, with Williams ready from late summer. The late and winter pears follow into autumn, and like all European pears they are picked firm and ripened off the tree rather than left to soften on the branch.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Western Australia has a rare advantage: it is free of codling moth, the grub that is the worst pest of pears and apples almost everywhere else, so WA gardeners are spared the spraying their eastern counterparts need. Pear and cherry slug is present in the south-west and will skeletonise pear leaves, and the fruit fly to watch here is the Mediterranean fruit fly, which is endemic in WA and attacks both pears and apples. Australia as a whole is also free of fire blight, the serious bacterial disease of pears, and keeping it out is a national biosecurity priority.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine is part of why it stays free of codling moth and other pests, so pear trees and budwood from interstate must be permitted on the WA Organism List and are usually treated and certified before they can enter. Many eastern nurseries will not post live plants to WA as a result, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here.

faqs.q: Which pears suit Perth and the south-west?
faqs.a: In the cooler Perth Hills and the Southern Forests you can grow the full range of European pears, Williams, Packham's Triumph and Beurre Bosc among them. On the warm coastal plain and in the Swan Valley, where winters are milder, lean on nashi and the low-chill pears such as Flordahome and Hood.

faqs.q: Can pears cope with Perth's sandy coastal soils?
faqs.a: They can, with help. Pears prefer a deep soil, so dig in plenty of compost before planting, mulch thickly, use a soil wetting agent if the sand has gone water-repellent, and feed little and often because nutrients leach quickly through sand. Nashi are the more forgiving choice near the coast, and planting up in the hills buys you both better soil and more chill.

QLD.intro: Growing pears in Queensland. Pears want a cold winter, so European pears are a Granite Belt crop in the cool south, while the warm coast and subtropics grow nashi and low-chill pears instead.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Most of Queensland is too warm in winter to chill European pears properly, so they are a crop for the cool, elevated south. The standout is the Granite Belt, high enough to deliver real winter cold. Away from there, in subtropical south-east Queensland and along the coast, the pears to grow are nashi and the low-chill European types such as Flordahome and Hood, which fruit on only a few hundred chill hours.

sections.heading: Where pears grow in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's European pears come almost entirely from the Granite Belt around Stanthorpe, Applethorpe and Ballandean, near the New South Wales border. Sitting at 800 to 1000 metres, the Granite Belt has the highest winter chill in the state, with the frosty nights that pears and apples need, and grows them alongside stone fruit and grapes. Pear growing thins out quickly as you drop toward the warmer coast.

sections.heading: Nashi and low-chill pears for the subtropics
sections.body: In subtropical south-east Queensland the answer is the nashi. Low-chill nashi such as Shinseiki and the very low chill China pear fruit reliably on the coast and are recommended for home orchards there, grown much like a European pear but easier and tree-ripened. The low-chill European pears Flordahome and Hood will also crop, though both need a second tree to pollinate them, so plant a pair in the sunniest, most open spot you have.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: On the Granite Belt pears are picked through summer and into autumn, the European kinds firm and green for ripening indoors. In the warm subtropics the nashi ripen on the tree in autumn and are picked ready to eat.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic across the state and readily attacks pears, so backyard fruit usually needs exclusion bags, netting or a baiting program. Codling moth is present in the cold districts and is the main pest of the fruit there, pear scab can mark leaves and fruit in a wet season, and pear and cherry slug turns up on pears in the cooler areas.

faqs.q: Can you grow pears in Brisbane or coastal Queensland?
faqs.a: Yes, if you choose the right type. Nashi and the low-chill European pears such as Flordahome and Hood will fruit in Brisbane's mild winters, though they crop more lightly than the same trees on the Granite Belt. Pick the warmest, sunniest spot, plant two for pollination, and protect the fruit from Queensland fruit fly.

faqs.q: Which pears suit the Granite Belt?
faqs.a: Because the Granite Belt has a genuinely cold winter, it grows the full range of European pears: Williams, Packham's Triumph, Beurre Bosc and the winter keepers, the same varieties as the southern states. That cold winter is the whole reason Queensland's pears sit high in the south rather than near the coast.

NSW.intro: Growing pears in New South Wales. NSW grows pears in its cool tablelands rather than on the warm coast, and it holds a special place in pear history as the home of Packham's Triumph, the variety that became Australia's main pear.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: New South Wales grows pears wherever the winter is cold enough, which means the cool tablelands and elevated districts rather than the warm, humid coast. The high country supplies the chill that European pears need, while the coast is lower in chill and harder on the trees through humid, scab-prone springs, so it suits nashi and low-chill pears instead. Choose your variety to match your district.

sections.heading: Where pears grow in NSW
sections.body: The cool Central Tablelands around Orange are the main pear district, with Batlow on the Snowy Mountains' western slopes and Bilpin in the Blue Mountains also growing pome fruit. The variety that put the state on the pear map, though, came from a small property at Garra, just south-west of Molong.

sections.heading: The home of Packham's Triumph
sections.body: At Garra, near Molong, the orchardist Charles Henry Packham crossed an Uvedale's St Germain (the old Bell pear) with a Williams to breed a late-keeping pear of outstanding flavour and storage. Packham's Triumph went on to become Australia's main commercial pear, around 60 per cent of the national crop and a leading export, and it is grown across the southern hemisphere today. Like the Granny Smith apple, it is a reminder that a famous variety can begin as one grower's lucky seedling.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Early Williams are picked from about February, with the cool, high districts such as Orange and Batlow finishing later than the warmer areas. European pears are picked firm and green and ripened off the tree, while the winter pears are stored cold first.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Pear and cherry slug skeletonises pear leaves through the warmer months and is the commonest backyard pest, controlled by hosing or dusting. Codling moth attacks the fruit across the growing districts, Queensland fruit fly is established on the coast and inland and uses pears as a host, and pear scab is the main fungal worry in humid springs.

faqs.q: Which pears suit Sydney and the warm NSW coast?
faqs.a: Sydney's mild, humid winters are low in chill, so nashi and low-chill European pears such as Flordahome and Hood are the dependable picks for a coastal backyard, with lighter crops than you would get inland. Up in the cool tablelands you can grow the full range of European pears instead.

faqs.q: Are nashi pears a good choice for a Sydney backyard?
faqs.a: Very much so. They need less winter cold than European pears, several types fruit without a second tree, they have few pest and disease troubles, and you eat them crisp from the branch. For a small, low-fuss coastal garden they are hard to beat.

VIC.intro: Growing pears in Victoria. Victoria is Australia's pear state by a wide margin, with about 90 per cent of the nation's pears grown in the Goulburn Valley, the heartland of both the fresh and the canning pear trade.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The Goulburn Valley's cool, mild winters give pears the chill they need, and its long, sun-drenched summers ripen the fruit fully, which is exactly the combination pears want. Because pears blossom early, frost in a low-lying orchard can still nip the spring flowers, and the cooler, wetter districts to the south carry more pear scab in a damp season. Across most of the state, though, winter chill is plentiful and the limiting factor is summer water rather than cold.

sections.heading: Where pears grow in VIC
sections.body: The Goulburn Valley, around Shepparton, Tatura, Ardmona, Kyabram, Mooroopna and Cobram, grows the great majority of Australia's pears, helped by deep river-flat soils and irrigation from the Goulburn system. Cooler districts add to the spread: the Yarra Valley near Melbourne and the high country grow pears and nashi for fresh markets, and home trees do well right across the state.

sections.heading: The canning pear country
sections.body: Victoria's pear industry grew up around the cannery. Williams Bon Chretien, the same pear Americans call Bartlett, is superb fresh but also the classic bottling and canning pear, and it built the Goulburn Valley trade: the growers' cooperative SPC, the Shepparton Fruit Preserving Company, started in 1917 and canned the district's pears, peaches and nectarines for generations. Packham's Triumph, the late-keeping variety, is the other mainstay and the main fresh and export pear, making up around 60 per cent of the national crop to Williams' 20 and Beurre Bosc's 10.

sections.heading: Intensive orchards and the Tatura Trellis
sections.body: Victoria gave tree-fruit growing the Tatura Trellis, a high-density, V-shaped training system worked out at the Tatura research station in the Goulburn Valley to crop more fruit, sooner, from a given area. Home gardeners borrow the same idea on a small scale by training a pear flat as an espalier along a wall or wires, which suits a courtyard or a sunny fence and makes a heavy crop easy to net and reach.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Pears flower in spring and the crop comes in from late summer into autumn. Early Williams are picked from about February, firm and green, while Packham's Triumph and the winter pears such as Beurre Bosc follow later and are stored to ripen. As with all European pears, the fruit is picked hard and ripened off the tree.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Pear and cherry slug, the small dark sawfly larva that skeletonises pear leaves, is the most common backyard nuisance and is easily knocked back by hosing or dusting the foliage. Codling moth is present and is the main pest of the fruit itself, and pear scab can mark leaves and fruit in a wet spring. Queensland fruit fly has become established in parts of the state, including the Goulburn Murray Valley, so fruit fly control is now part of growing pears in many districts.

faqs.a: Almost all of them, since the winter chill is ample. Williams Bon Chretien, Packham's Triumph, Beurre Bosc and the winter-keeper Josephine all crop well for fresh eating and for bottling, and nashi do nicely too. In the cooler, damper southern districts, keep the canopy open and watch for scab in wet springs.

faqs.q: Do pears need more winter chill than apples?
faqs.a: Mainstream European pears need a genuinely cold winter, much like apples, which is why Victoria suits them so well. Nashi and the low-chill European pears such as Flordahome and Hood get by on far less, so they are the ones to choose in a mild pocket or a warm-summer inland garden where the cold does not really bite.
